title: THE UNSELFISH GENE
content_html:
The Unselfish Gene

A psychologist gives his view on how humans became self-centred

There has long been a general assumption that human beings are essentially selfish. We're apparently ruthless, with strong impulses to compete against each other for resources and to accumulate power and possessions. If we are kind to one another, it's usually because we have ulterior motives. If we are good, it's only because we have managed to control and transcend our innate selfishness and brutality.

This bleak view of human nature is closely associated with the science writer Richard Dawkins, whose 1976 book The Selfish Gene became popular because it fitted so well with - and helped to justify - the competitive and individualistic ethos that was so prevalent in late 20th-century societies. Like many others, Dawkins justifies his views with reference to the field of evolutionary psychology. Evolutionary psychology theorises that present-day human traits developed in prehistoric times, during what is termed the 'environment of evolutionary adaptedness'.

Prehistory is usually seen as a period of intense competition, when life was such a brutal battle that only those with traits such as selfishness, aggression and ruthlessness survived. And because survival depended on access to resources - such as rivers, forests and animals - there was bound to be conflict between rival groups, which led to the development of traits such as racism and warfare. This seems logical. But, in fact, the assumption on which this all rests - that prehistoric life was a desperate struggle for survival - is false.

It's important to remember that in the prehistoric era, the world was very sparsely populated. According to some estimates, around 15,000 years ago, the population of Europe was only 29,000, and the population of the whole world was less than half a million. Humans at that time were hunter-gatherers: people who lived by hunting wild animals and collecting wild plants. With such small population densities, it seems unlikely that prehistoric hunter-gatherer groups had to compete against each other for resources or had any need to develop ruthlessness and competitiveness, or to go to war.

There is significant evidence to back this notion from contemporary hunter-gatherer groups, who live in the same way as prehistoric humans did. As the anthropologist Bruce Knauft has remarked, hunter-gatherers are characterised by 'extreme political and sexual egalitarianism'. Knauft has observed that individuals in such groups don't accumulate property or possessions and have an ethical obligation to share everything. They also have methods of preserving egalitarianism by ensuring that disparities of status don't arise.

The Kung people of southern Africa, for example, swap arrows before going hunting and when an animal is killed, the acclaim does not go to the person who fired the arrow, but to the person the arrow belongs to. And if a person becomes too domineering, the other members of the group ostracise them, exiling the offender from society. Typically in such groups, men do not dictate what women do. Women in hunter-gatherer groups worldwide often benefit from a high level of autonomy, being able to select their own marriage partners, decide what work they do and work whenever they choose to. And if a marriage breaks down, they have custody rights over their children.

Many anthropologists believe that societies such as the Kung were normal until a few thousand years ago, when population growth led to the development of agriculture and a settled lifestyle. In view of the above, there seems little reason to assume that traits such as racism, warfare and male domination should have been selected by evolution - as they would have been of little benefit in the prehistoric era. Individuals who behaved selfishly and ruthlessly would be less likely to survive, since they would have been ostracised from their groups.

It makes more sense, then, to see traits such as cooperation, egalitarianism, altruism and peacefulness as innate characteristics of human beings. These were the traits that were prevalent in human life for tens of thousands of years. So presumably these traits are still strong in us now.

But if prehistoric life wasn't really as brutal as has often been assumed, why do modern humans behave so selfishly and ruthlessly? Perhaps these negative traits should be seen as a later development, the result of environmental and psychological factors. Research has shown repeatedly that when the natural habitats of primates such as apes and gorillas are disrupted, they tend to become more violent and hierarchical.

So, it could well be that the same thing has happened to us. I believe that the end of the hunter-gatherer lifestyle and the advent of farming was connected to a psychological change that occurred in some groups of people. There was a new sense of individuality and separateness, which led to a new selfishness, and ultimately to hierarchical societies, patriarchy and warfare. At any rate, these negative traits appear to have developed so recently that it doesn't seem feasible to explain them in adaptive or evolutionary terms.

questions.text: What is the writer doing in the first paragraph?
options: setting out two opposing views about human nature
justifying his opinion about our tendency to be greedy
describing a commonly held belief about people's behaviour
explaining why he thinks that humans act in a selfish manner
questions.question: What is the writer doing in the first paragraph?
questions.explanation:
Câu hỏi:
“What is the writer doing in the first paragraph?”

Đoạn đầu liệt kê hàng loạt niềm tin phổ biến:

humans are selfish
ruthless
competitive
only kind for hidden motives

→ writer đang mô tả một quan điểm mà nhiều người tin về bản chất con người, chưa đưa phản biện ngay.

→ Đáp án C: describing a commonly held belief.

_blockConfig.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.question_text: 27 What is writer doing in first paragraph?

questions.text: What point is made about Richard Dawkins' book The Selfish Gene?
options: Its appeal lay in the radical nature of its ideas.
Its success was due to the scientific support it offered.
It presented a view that was in line with the attitudes of its time.
It took an innovative approach to the analysis of human psychology.
questions.question: What point is made about Richard Dawkins' book The Selfish Gene?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword

Dawkins
Selfish Gene
popularity
society attitude

Bước 2: Đoạn liên quan

Đoạn 2 nói cuốn sách trở nên phổ biến vì nó phù hợp với tinh thần cạnh tranh và cá nhân chủ nghĩa của xã hội cuối thế kỷ 20.

Bước 3: Phân tích

Câu hỏi hỏi vì sao cuốn sách thành công.
Bài không nói sách được ủng hộ vì bằng chứng khoa học mạnh hay vì ý tưởng quá mới. Thay vào đó, nó được chấp nhận rộng rãi vì phù hợp với tư tưởng và giá trị xã hội thời điểm đó. Đây là yếu tố xã hội và văn hóa, không phải yếu tố khoa học.

Đáp án C
questions.question_text: 28 What does writer suggest about Dawkins?

questions.text: What does the writer suggest about the prehistoric era in the fourth paragraph?
options: Societies were more complex than many people believe.
Supplies of natural resources were probably relatively plentiful.
Most estimates about population sizes are likely to be inaccurate.
Humans moved across continents more than was previously thought.
questions.question: What does the writer suggest about the prehistoric era in the fourth paragraph?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword

prehistoric era
population
resources
competition

Bước 2: Đoạn liên quan

Đoạn 4 nói dân số thời tiền sử rất thấp và con người sống thành nhóm nhỏ săn bắt hái lượm, nên không có cạnh tranh tài nguyên giữa các nhóm như thường được giả định.

Bước 3: Phân tích

Câu hỏi yêu cầu suy ra quan điểm của tác giả về thời tiền sử.
Tác giả bác bỏ quan niệm rằng đây là thời kỳ đấu tranh khốc liệt để giành tài nguyên. Vì dân số thấp và nhóm người sống rải rác, nên áp lực cạnh tranh tài nguyên không lớn như người ta thường nghĩ. Trong các lựa chọn, phương án phù hợp nhất là tài nguyên tương đối dồi dào hoặc ít gây cạnh tranh.

Đáp án B
questions.question_text: 29 what is writer doing in third paragraph?

questions.text: The writer refers to Bruce Knauft's work as support for the idea that
options: selfishness is a relatively recent development in human societies.
only people in isolated communities can live in an unselfish manner.
very few lifestyles have survived unchanged since prehistoric times.
hunter-gatherer cultures worldwide are declining in number.
questions.question: The writer refers to Bruce Knauft's work as support for the idea that
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword

Bruce Knauft
hunter gatherers
support argument

Bước 2: Đoạn liên quan

Đoạn 5 mô tả các xã hội săn bắt hái lượm có tính bình đẳng cao, không tích lũy tài sản và có nghĩa vụ chia sẻ tài nguyên.

Bước 3: Phân tích

Câu hỏi hỏi Knauft được dùng để hỗ trợ ý tưởng gì.
Những bằng chứng ông đưa ra cho thấy các xã hội nguyên thủy không mang tính cạnh tranh và ích kỷ cao như giả định tiến hóa học phổ biến. Điều này hỗ trợ lập luận rằng các đặc điểm như ích kỷ và bạo lực không phải là bản chất nguyên thủy của con người.

Đáp án A
questions.question_text: 30 what is writer doing in fourth paragraph?

questions.text: Bruce Knauft's research shows that contemporary hunter-gatherer societies tend to exhibit a high level of ______in all areas of life.
questions.question: Bruce Knauft's research shows that contemporary hunter-gatherer societies tend to exhibit a high level of ______in all areas of life.
questions.groupHtml:
Questions 31–35

Complete the summary below.

Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questions.explanation:
Câu:
“high level of ___ in all areas of life”

Đoạn:
“extreme political and sexual egalitarianism”

→ egalitarianism = equality.

→ Đáp án: egalitarianism.

_blockConfig.noteContent:
**Contemporary hunter-gatherer societies**
Bruce Knauft’s research shows that contemporary hunter-gatherer societies tend to exhibit a high level of [31] in all areas of life. In these cultures, distributing resources fairly among all members is a moral obligation. These societies also employ strategies to prevent differences in [32] occurring: for example, the IKung follow a custom whereby the credit for one person’s success at [33] is given to another member of the group. Individuals who behave in a [34] manner are punished by being excluded from the group, and women have a considerable amount of [35] in choices regarding work and marriage.

questions.questionHtml:
Contemporary hunter-gatherer societies

Bruce Knauft’s research shows that contemporary hunter-gatherer societies tend to exhibit a high level of 31_______ in all areas of life. In these cultures, distributing resources fairly among all members is a moral obligation. These societies also employ strategies to prevent differences in 32_______ occurring: for example, the IKung follow a custom whereby the credit for one person’s success at 33_______ is given to another member of the group. Individuals who behave in a 34_______ manner are punished by being excluded from the group, and women have a considerable amount of 35_______ in choices regarding work and marriage.
questions.question_text: 31 hunter-gatherer groups characterized by political and sexual ___________

questions.text: These societies also employ strategies to prevent differences in ______ occurring: for example,
questions.question: These societies also employ strategies to prevent differences in ______ occurring: for example,
questions.explanation:
Câu:
“prevent differences in ___ occurring”

Đoạn:
“ensuring that disparities of status don't arise”

→ disparities = differences.

→ Đáp án: status.

questions.text: the !Kung follow a custom whereby the credit for one person's success at ______ is given to another member of the group.
questions.question: the !Kung follow a custom whereby the credit for one person's success at ______ is given to another member of the group.
questions.explanation:
Câu:
“credit for one person's success at ___”

Đoạn:
“swap arrows before going hunting”

→ when animal killed, praise goes to arrow owner.

→ success in hunting credited to someone else.

→ Đáp án: hunting.
questions.question_text: 33 acclaim goes to person ___________ belongs to

questions.text: Individuals who behave in a ______ manner are punished by being excluded from the group
questions.question: Individuals who behave in a ______ manner are punished by being excluded from the group
questions.explanation:
Câu:
“Individuals who behave in a ___ manner”

Đoạn:
“if a person becomes too domineering”

→ domineering people are ostracised/excluded.

→ Đáp án: domineering.

questions.text: and women have a considerable amount of ______ in choices regarding work and marriage.
questions.question: and women have a considerable amount of ______ in choices regarding work and marriage.
questions.explanation:
Câu:
“women have a considerable amount of ___”

Đoạn:
“Women ... benefit from a high level of autonomy”

→ autonomy = freedom/independence.

→ Đáp án: autonomy.

questions.text: Some anthropologists are mistaken about the point when the number of societies such as the !Kung began to decline.
questions.question: Some anthropologists are mistaken about the point when the number of societies such as the !Kung began to decline.
questions.groupHtml:
Questions 36–40

Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in the Reading Passage?

In boxes 36–40 on your answer sheet, write
YES  if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer
NO  if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer
NOT GIVEN  if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this
questions.explanation:
Statement:
“Some anthropologists are mistaken...”

Bài chỉ nói:
“Many anthropologists believe...”

→ không nói belief đó đúng hay sai.

→ NOT GIVEN.

_blockConfig.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.question_text: 36 women have ___________ over children if marriage breaks

questions.text: Humans who developed warlike traits in prehistory would have had an advantage over those who did not.
questions.question: Humans who developed warlike traits in prehistory would have had an advantage over those who did not.
questions.explanation:
Statement:
“Humans with warlike traits would have had an advantage”

Đoạn:
“Individuals who behaved selfishly and ruthlessly would be less likely to survive”

→ opposite meaning:

statement = advantage
passage = disadvantage

→ NO.
questions.question_text: 37 selfish and ruthless would be less likely to survive.

questions.text: Being peaceful and cooperative is a natural way for people to behave.
questions.question: Being peaceful and cooperative is a natural way for people to behave.
questions.explanation:
Statement:
“Being peaceful and cooperative is natural”

Đoạn:
“cooperation, egalitarianism, altruism and peacefulness” are “innate characteristics”

→ innate = natural/inborn.

→ YES.
questions.question_text: 38 environmental and psychological factors changed human nature.

questions.text: Negative traits are more apparent in some modern cultures than in others.
questions.question: Negative traits are more apparent in some modern cultures than in others.
questions.explanation:
Statement:
“Negative traits are more apparent in some cultures”

Bài không hề so sánh culture này với culture khác.

→ NOT GIVEN.
questions.question_text: 39 psychological changes after end of hunter-gatherer lifestyle.

questions.text: Animal research has failed to reveal a link between changes in the environment and the emergence of aggressive tendencies.
questions.question: Animal research has failed to reveal a link between changes in the environment and the emergence of aggressive tendencies.
questions.explanation:
Statement:
“Animal research has failed to reveal a link...”

Đoạn:
“when habitats ... are disrupted, they tend to become more violent”

→ research DID reveal a link between environment change and aggression.

→ statement trái nghĩa hoàn toàn.

→ NO.
questions.question_text: 40 patriarchy and warfare can be explained in adaptive terms.
